era_context: Early Iron Age II in the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The kingdoms of Israel and Judah are firmly established as separate political entities. Egypt's 22nd Dynasty continues. Phoenician maritime networks are expanding. Assyria remains relatively weak. This period is characterized by regional stability and the consolidation of independent kingdoms in the Levant.

events.title: Rehoboam of Judah continues reign; possible period of Egyptian campaign aftermath
events.description: Rehoboam's reign continues in Judah. If Shoshenq I's campaign is dated to 925 BCE (as some scholars propose), 926 BCE would fall immediately before this major event. Alternatively, if the campaign occurred earlier (c. 930 BCE) or later (c. 920 BCE), 926 BCE would fall within the period of recovery or anticipation. The kingdom continues to consolidate its political and administrative structures following the schism of the United Monarchy.

sources.name: Archaeological survey of Judahite Iron Age II sites

events.certainty_note: Rehoboam's reign is traditionally dated c. 930–913 BCE. No specific events are recorded for 926 BCE. The dating of Shoshenq I's campaign remains disputed, with estimates ranging from 945 BCE to 920 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of Judahite political consolidation

events.title: Jeroboam I's reign continues; northern Kingdom of Israel maintains stability
events.description: Jeroboam I continues to rule the northern Kingdom of Israel. Archaeological evidence from northern Levantine sites shows continued settlement and economic activity. The kingdom maintains control of important trade routes and agricultural lands. No major military or political crises are recorded for this year.

sources.name: Archaeological survey of Iron Age II northern Levantine sites

events.certainty_note: Jeroboam's reign is traditionally dated c. 930–909 BCE. No specific events are recorded for 926 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are recorded for 926 BCE in surviving sources. The chronological framework for this period is based on biblical genealogies and Egyptian king lists, both of which have significant uncertainties. The dating of Shoshenq I's campaign, which may have occurred near this year, is one of the most disputed issues in ancient Near Eastern chronology.
historiographic_note: For 926 BCE, the historical record is essentially silent. No contemporary sources document specific events for this year. The entries above describe the general context of the reigns of Rehoboam and Jeroboam I. The chronological framework itself is a modern scholarly construction with substantial uncertainties.

graph_edges.note: Rehoboam's reign continues toward the possible date of Shoshenq I's campaign (925 BCE)
